VO_EN: The first time, you look at them in the mirror.
Now you turn the radio up when they cry.
Twelve hours a day, sometimes fourteen.
You know every road in this city.
You know which streets flood in the rain.
You know which passengers want to talk and which ones don't.
That's the only skill that matters.
The woman on Tuesdays who sits behind you and talks about her son.
The old man with the cane who always tips too much.
They haven't been enough for a while.
A man you've never met sits in the back one afternoon.
He doesn't give you a destination.
He says: when it buzzes, you drive.
He says: you don't ask where.
You count it later in the car.
It's more than you make in a month.
You clip the phone to your belt next to the other one.
You kiss your daughter's forehead before you leave.
Her room smells like soap and laundry detergent.
You whisper: I'll be back by morning.
You don't know if that's true.
But it sounds like something a father says.
The phone buzzes at eleven p.m.
You don't know what it means yet.
You rent a sedan from the lot downtown.
A person gets in at the bus station.
They sit in the back and say nothing.
They're wearing thin clothes for how cold it is outside.
The word forms in your throat.
Something like: where are we going?
But that's their question. Not yours.
That is the first time you choose not to know.
The headlights cut a narrow tunnel through the dark.
The person in the backseat breathes.
They ask: where are you taking me?
Like they already know you won't answer.
They try to say something else.
You can see it in the way they hold their hands.
You tilt it until you can only see the road behind you.
You drive five under the limit.
You grip the wheel at ten and two.
Your hands tighten on the wheel.
You say: my cousin is visiting a doctor in the city.
Your hands don't unclench for another two miles.
The steering wheel feels damp under your palms.
You just know what happens if you're late.
You are not racing the road.
You say yes before he finishes the sentence.
That's more than a month of taxi rides.
More than a month of conversations.
More than a month of tips from the old man with the cane.
An hour later, someone brings a cooler.
You put a towel on the backseat.
You place the cooler on the towel.
You set the AC to eighteen degrees.
The phone told you that once.
You know the curve where the asphalt dips.
You know the pothole where the cooler slides.
You steer around it without thinking.
There's a receipt on the dashboard from last time.
You don't remember leaving it there.
The drop-off is a parking lot behind a warehouse.
You sit in the empty car for a minute.
Same angle as the rearview mirror.
There's a phone number at the bottom.
You reach back with one hand.
Your other hand stays on the wheel.
You don't remember the next twenty minutes.
You're checking if the cooler shifted.
You get home at four a.m.
The light comes through the crack.
She always leaves it on when you're not home.
You put your hand on the door handle.
Your hands still smell like the steering wheel.
You teach him which gas stations have no cameras.
You tell him: don't look in the mirror too long.
The man who gave you the phone.
Don't say thank you when they get out.
You used to talk to passengers.
You used to ask where they were going.
You don't remember when that stopped.
Or if it stopped all at once.
Or just a little at a time.
You used to look in the mirror.
You used to see a face.
You don't adjust the mirror anymore.
There is nothing back there you need to see.
